skill.name: plan-directory
skill.description: Create and maintain markdown plan directories with a master PLAN.md index and numbered task files. The skill defines task structure, dependencies, checklists, tests, blockers, and completion criteria.
skill.summary: Build structured project plan directories with indexed task files and measurable completion tracking.
skill.author: DiversioTeam

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, traversal, crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found they are primarily markdown examples, inline filenames, template paths, and authentication examples. The remaining real risk is operational: the skill is allowed to read, write, edit, glob, and use Bash while creating plan directories, so users should review target paths before execution.

medium_findings.title: Broad Filesystem Modification Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill is explicitly allowed to read, write, edit, and glob files, and its workflow creates and updates PLAN.md and numbered task files. This is legitimate for the skill, but incorrect target paths could modify unintended project files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tools and workflow directly show file read and write behavior. The purpose is benign, but the capability can affect repository files.

medium_findings.title: Bash Permission for a Planning Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash as an allowed tool and includes examples of runnable commands in task tests. This does not show command injection or malware, but it increases execution risk compared with a documentation-only skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Bash access is explicitly declared and command examples are present. No evidence shows hidden or automatic command execution.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The repeated static matches for Ruby or shell backtick execution occur in markdown inline code and fenced templates, such as filenames, checkbox examples, and installation commands. They are not executable source code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is markdown documentation, not Ruby, shell, or a script file. The surrounding context describes templates and examples.

low_findings.title: Cryptography and Network Terms Are Example Content
low_findings.description: Static high-risk crypto and reconnaissance matches map to planning examples that mention JWT, OAuth, login tests, and security scans. No implementation code, secret handling, or outbound network request is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are explanatory examples inside a planning template. I found no code that implements weak cryptography or performs network reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Relative Sub-Plan Link Is Documentation
low_findings.description: The path traversal alert is a relative markdown reference to a sibling plan directory. It documents how to link a sub-plan and does not itself read, write, or execute that path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a fenced markdown note example for sub-plans. The surrounding steps describe documentation structure, not traversal-based file access.

dangerous_patterns.title: File and Command Tooling Requires User Path Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines file modification tools with Bash permission. This is expected for scaffolding plans, but users should confirm destination directories and commands before allowing changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool permissions and workflow are explicit. The risk is capability-based rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

content.user_title: Organize Project Plans Into Task Directories
content.value_statement: Complex work often loses context when plans live in one long note. This skill creates a clear PLAN.md index with numbered task files, dependencies, tests, and completion checks.
actual_capabilities: Creates a master PLAN.md file for a project, feature, or migration.
Creates numbered task files using the NNN-slug.md naming pattern.
Defines required task sections for goals, dependencies, scope, checklists, tests, and completion criteria.
Updates existing plans by adding tasks, marking progress, or tracking blockers.
Keeps PLAN.md task status aligned with completed task files.
Documents parallel task relationships and blocked work.
limitations: It does not execute the project work described in the plan.
It needs a clear plan title, slug, and task list to produce useful files.
It can modify repository files, so target directories should be reviewed first.
It does not validate dependency graphs with a separate parser.

use_cases.title: Plan a Feature Build
use_cases.description: Break a product feature into numbered tasks with dependencies, tests, and completion checks.

use_cases.title: Coordinate a Migration
use_cases.description: Create a repeatable migration plan that separates setup, implementation, validation, and rollout work.

use_cases.title: Track AI-Assisted Work
use_cases.description: Give an AI coding agent a structured directory it can update as checklist items complete.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new plan directory.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a plan directory for [plan title]. Use the slug [plan-slug]. Include these tasks: [task list].

prompt_templates.title: Add Testing Detail
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan creation with explicit validation expectations.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a structured plan for [project]. Each task should include tests. Use these commands or QA checks: [testing expectations].

prompt_templates.title: Update an Existing Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Maintain a plan that already has PLAN.md and task files.
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the existing plan at [path]. Add a new task for [task]. Preserve existing task numbers and update the PLAN.md index.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Parallel Work Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for multiple contributors or agents.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a plan for [project] with dependencies and parallel task markers. Identify which tasks can run at the same time and which tasks must wait.

output_examples.input: Plan a user authentication feature with database setup, login endpoints, password reset, and tests.
output_examples.output: A plan directory with PLAN.md plus separate numbered task files for user model setup, authentication configuration, login endpoints, password reset, and integration tests.

output_examples.input: Add Google OAuth to an existing user authentication plan.
output_examples.output: The existing plan gains the next numbered task file, and PLAN.md receives a matching unchecked task entry without renumbering prior work.

output_examples.input: Mark the JWT setup task as completed.
output_examples.output: The task checklist, tests, and completion criteria are checked, and the matching PLAN.md task index item is updated.

best_practices: Provide a clear title, slug, target path, and task list before creating files.
Keep each task small enough to finish in one focused work session.
Review the target directory before allowing the skill to write or update files.
anti_patterns: Use it for a short personal todo list that does not need files.
Ask it to renumber tasks after work has started.
Accept vague checklist items without measurable tests or completion criteria.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates a PLAN.md index and numbered markdown task files for structured project planning.

faq.question: Can it update an existing plan?
faq.answer: Yes. It reads the current PLAN.md, preserves task numbers, and adds or updates targeted task files.

faq.question: Does it run the tasks in the plan?
faq.answer: No. It organizes the plan and progress tracking. Separate work is needed to complete the tasks.

faq.question: Why does it need file access?
faq.answer: It writes PLAN.md and task files, then edits them when progress or scope changes.

faq.question: Is Bash required for every use?
faq.answer: No. Bash is allowed, but many planning tasks only need file reads and edits.

faq.question: What tools support this skill?
faq.answer: The skill states support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
